courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: At least 50% for NSC Geography, or GEO1009F. For Humanities students: at least 50% for NSC Geography.
courses.course_outline: The course presents fundamental environmental and geographical concepts from the fields of human and physical geography, and explores the interconnections, flows and boundaries across these systems. How these systems have played out in the past, how they are changing, and what the future may hold is critical to a full appreciation of current and future environmental challenges. The course brings together an understanding of both the biophysical geographic settings and processes that inform all facets of our landscapes, and the social engagement with their components, which present both opportunity and limitation to human development.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Tutorials/Practicals: One per every two weeks, Monday, Tuesday or Thursday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance of all, and satisfactory completion of all tutorial and practical assignments; students must attain an average mark of not less than 40% for the coursework component.
courses.assessment: Essays, assignments and tutorial work count 60%; one 2-hour theory examination written in November counts 40% (sub-minimum of 40% required).

courses.course_title: Geosystems: An Introduction to Earth & Environmental Sciences
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science (administered by Geological Sciences)
courses.prerequisites: At least 50% for NSC Geography, or at least 60% for NSC Physical Science or Life Sciences. Preference given to students majoring in Archaeology, Environmental and Geographical Science, Geology or Oceanography.
courses.course_outline: This course aims to develop a broad understanding of how the Earth works. Over its >4.5 billion-year history, the Earth has been shaped by chemical, physical and biological processes. The course demonstrates why decoding the rock record of deep-time events is critical for explaining past, present and future environmental changes and the distribution of natural resources. It examines the interplay of terrestrial, marine, atmospheric and cryospheric processes within the Earth system, with emphasis on monitoring essential variables for understanding climate variability and change. The course also reviews human evolution and how Homo sapiens became the dominant cause of global environmental change, and explores social risks and vulnerabilities in response to the Earth's biophysical processes. It concludes with concepts of environmental hazard, risk, resilience, monitoring and adaptation.
courses.notes: Presented jointly by the Departments of Archaeology, Environmental & Geographical Sciences, Geological Sciences, and Oceanography, but administered by Geological Sciences. Students majoring in these subjects are required to attend one half-day excursion.
courses.dp_requirements: An average of 40% on all marked classwork and tests. Submission of 80% of weekly assignments in each of the four sections (GEO, AGE, SEA, EGS). Attendance of the field trip is compulsory for students majoring in Archaeology, EGS, Geology or Oceanography.
courses.assessment: Marked classwork including class test/s counts 50%; a 3-hour written examination counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% in the class mark is required to write the theory examination. Supplementary examinations offered in July.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: For BSc: GEO1009F or EGS1007S or EGS1003S. For BA or BSocSci: EGS1007S or EGS1003S or Social Science Foundation course and two full first-year Humanities courses, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: Environmental and geographical science is about the relationships in place and across space and time. This course focuses on spatial dimensions. Understanding the social, human, physical and biophysical interactions is critical to addressing current environmental challenges. The course introduces students to a wide range of geographical concepts through themes such as historical legacies, boundaries, conservation and environmental governance.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 5th period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Friday, 14h00–17h00, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Submission of all practical assignments and essay; sub-minimum of 45%.
courses.assessment: Two essays (10% each), two class tests (5% each), two field-based exercises (5% each), one 2-hour exam (60%). An average mark of at least 50% across all submissions is required to pass the course.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: For BSc: GEO1009F or EGS1007S or EGS1003S. For BA or BSocSci: EGS1007S or EGS1003S or Social Science Foundation course and two full first-year Humanities courses, or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: This course focuses on temporal dimensions. Interwoven social and biophysical patterns are explored with a view to informing just and sustainable transitions. Historical legacies, future forecasting, governance, climate variability and climate change are examined and understood in their contribution to current trajectories. The course engages temporal scales from geological epochs through to socio-political historical legacies and the variable lived experiences of the present. It offers critiques of northern-derived linear notions of time and scalar assumptions in global governance, and introduces students to interdisciplinary conversation through engaging geographical thought and praxis around emergent and interrelated social and biophysical patterns.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 5th period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Monday, 14h00–17h00, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Submission of all practical assignments and essay; sub-minimum of 45%.
courses.assessment: 60% exam; 40% coursework (2 essays – 10% each, two class tests – 5% each, two field-based exercises – 5% each). An average mark of at least 50% across all submissions is required to pass the course.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: At least 60% in NSC Mathematics; GEO1009F or equivalent; EGS2016F (or EGS2013F) and/or EGS2017S (or EGS2015S) or SEA2004F or any approved 2000-level Science course; and/or any approved 1000-level Physics or Mathematics course.
courses.course_outline: This course provides a thorough understanding of the physical processes that control the Earth's atmosphere. Topics include: atmospheric energy balance, thermodynamics, dynamics and general circulation; tropical and mid-latitude weather producing systems; weather and climate extreme events (e.g. heat-waves, drought, floods) in Africa; climate variability and change; atmospheric boundary layer turbulence, chemistry and pollution. Lectures are complemented with field measurements and laboratory practicals demonstrating basic data analysis techniques in atmospheric sciences.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 1st period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Tuesday or Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory completion of practicals and all written assignments, including essays, project reports and class tests.
courses.assessment: Essays and tests count 20%; project reports and practicals count 20%; one 3-hour examination in November counts 60% (sub-minimum of 40% required).

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS2016F (or EGS2013F), EGS2017S (or EGS2015S).
courses.course_outline: The course critically engages with current debates and discourses in the fields of sustainability, vulnerability and environmental management, including examination of key concepts such as integration, systems-thinking, complexity, equity, vulnerability, risk, resilience, adaptation and mitigation. Approaches and methods for analysing environmental problems and integrating risk reduction, as well as sustainability principles and practices into policy, programme, plan and project cycle processes are investigated and applied in different contexts.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 3rd period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Compulsory fieldwork component involving a half-day field excursion.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance and satisfactory completion of practicals (including fieldwork), other assignments and tests; students must attain an average mark of not less than 40% for the coursework.
courses.assessment: Practical reports (including fieldwork), class tests and other assignments count 60%; one 3-hour June examination counts 40% (sub-minimum of 40% required).

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS2016F (or EGS2013F) and/or EGS2017S (or EGS2015S).
courses.course_outline: The course focuses on debates in classical and contemporary human geography. It considers important thematic areas in the geographical literature such as development, spatiality, urban, political and feminist geographies. Each thematic area explores specific debates and key authors' work, providing students with an introduction to the literature, a content overview, and skills to deconstruct and build conceptual and analytical arguments related to evidence drawn from geographical research from around the world (other than South Africa). The course also emphasises academic reading and writing skills taught in practical sessions.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 4th period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory completion of essay and practical assignments and participation in tutorials; students must attain an average mark of not less than 40% for the coursework.
courses.assessment: Essay and other assignments count 70%; one 3-hour written examination in November counts 30% (sub-minimum of 40% required).

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS2016F (or EGS2013F) and/or EGS2017S (or EGS2015S).
courses.course_outline: The course deals with the dynamic physical environment including the human impact on global environments at various spatial and temporal scales during the so-called Anthropocene. The general aim is to illustrate the nature and scale of changes that characterise the Earth's environment, against a background of both natural and anthropogenically-induced processes. This provides an important perspective when thinking about contemporary environments and how they might change in the future, with consequences for our own species and others with which we share the planet.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 5th period. Practicals: One afternoon per week, Thursday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory completion of practicals and all written assignments, including fieldwork report, essays and class tests. Students must attain an average mark of not less than 40% for the coursework.
courses.assessment: Field report, essays, class tests and practical assignments count 50%; one 3-hour examination written in June counts 50% (sub-minimum of 40% required).

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: As for EGS4004W, with the additional requirement of at least a half-course in Mathematics or a full-course in Physics, and a senior undergraduate course in climatology or atmospheric science. Computer literacy is highly recommended.
courses.course_outline: The Atmospheric Science programme provides a 4th year of development for those interested in following a career associated with atmospheric science and climatology, or for progression to research. The focus is on practical skills and the application of theory to issues related to the climate system. The programme follows the same pattern as EGS4004W, with the constraint that three of four course modules must be from the atmospheric options and the fourth from one of the Honours-level physical science options in EGS or Oceanography. Requirements include a research project, two seminar presentations, course fieldwork, and attendance and presentation at the annual conference of the South African Society for Atmospheric Scientists.
courses.notes: Course code EGS4001W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for EGS4052W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and EGS4053W (research project, 40 NQF credits).
courses.dp_requirements: Students must pass at least three of their coursework electives and achieve a composite pass on the coursework. Students must achieve a pass on their research project to proceed to graduation.
courses.assessment: Examinations follow the same structure as EGS4004W. Not all course options have formal examinations; a significant portion may be based on project tasks. Combined module results count 75% and the research project counts 25%. Project component must be passed separately for award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: A BSc degree with a major in Environmental & Geographical Science or related field. Acceptance at the discretion of the Head of Department, based on quality of final year results, undergraduate curriculum and referee reports. Preference may be given to UCT graduates. Entrance limited to 30 students.
courses.course_outline: Students complete four advanced semester modules, one of which must be a research methods module. Students complete a research methods course and then select three further modules from a range of advanced courses in Environmental and Geographical Science in Human Geography, Environmental Management or Physical Geography. Curricula must be approved by the course convener in consultation with the Head of Department. Each student also completes a research project. At the convener's discretion, students may take one course from outside the Department (in addition to the methods course) towards the degree.
courses.notes: Course code EGS4004W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for EGS4054W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and EGS4055W (research project, 40 NQF credits).
courses.dp_requirements: Students must pass at least three of their coursework electives and achieve a composite pass on the coursework. Students must achieve a pass on their research project to proceed to graduation.
courses.assessment: Combined module results (examinations, project, essay, fieldwork and seminar presentation marks) count 75%; research project counts 25%. Project component must be passed separately for award of the degree.

courses.course_title: Quantitative Research Methods for Environmental Earth Sciences
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS3012S or equivalent; OR at least one of a first-year Mathematics, Applied Mathematics, Physics or Computer Science course; OR at the discretion of the HOD.
courses.course_outline: The course has a dual purpose. Firstly, a series of weekly lectures and hands-on practical seminars covering the nuts and bolts of quantitative analysis, including fundamental methods commonly found in the literature: classification, time series analysis, EOF/PCA and non-linear analysis. In parallel, a series of seminars on the Philosophy of Science addresses issues of values, perception and the science community.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS4023F and EGS3012S or equivalent; OR at the discretion of the HoD.
courses.course_outline: The course provides an introduction to the fundamental elements of climate models and to the application of operational climate models and climate impact models to real-world questions, with applications focussed on the southern African context. Classes are held in the EGS computer lab, primarily using Python on Linux. Basic climate models — energy balance and advection-diffusion models — are developed from conceptual models to numerical implementation run on desktops.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Introduction to Climate Change & Sustainable Development
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Honours and by agreement of the course convenor.
courses.course_outline: This course provides a broad, integrated knowledge on key issues in climate change and sustainable development, making students conversant across the spectrum of climate change issues and history. Topics include: sustainable development; the climate system, anthropogenic forcing and climate system response; African climate variability and change; international climate change legal frameworks, negotiations and politics; the economics of climate change and climate change financing; and the concept of climate compatible development. The course is lecture, seminar and group-work based, with each section involving basic framing lectures supported by essay or group work exercises and seminars.
courses.dp_requirements: 80% of classes and all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.convener: Drs M Norton and D Sparks
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Honours and by agreement of the course convenor.
courses.course_outline: This course provides in-depth coverage of (i) adaptation and (ii) mitigation from both a theoretical and practical/applied point of view. Adaptation and mitigation are the two key domains of academic and applied learning required for students to be qualified to undertake research and be employable in the climate change arena in the South African and developing country context. Issues are explored from a developing country, climate compatible perspective.
courses.dp_requirements: 80% of classes and all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS. Note: Enrolment to this course is by invitation only.
courses.course_outline: South African landscapes have always been contested. In the contemporary moment of global environmental crisis and deepening social inequality, this course builds an interdisciplinary understanding of the histories, legacies and transformations playing out in particular places. Through foundational theoretical engagements and field-based activities, students explore cross-cutting social, political, economic, ecological and biophysical dynamics playing out in the context of the rapidly changing Southern African region.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The course explores the theory of climate change, then goes into the question of predictability, cross-scale relationships and feedbacks in the climate system, the tools and techniques of prediction, and translation of predictions into the user community including impacts and vulnerability analyses, touching on the social dimension.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Topics include an overview of poverty and urbanization in Southern Africa; urban food security, methods and issues; urban poverty and vulnerability debates; food security and health; managing urban food systems (ecological, regulatory and fiscal dynamics).
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Issues and themes in contemporary aspects of the Human/Environmental interface will be covered. Specific attention will be given to profiling core debates in a specialist field of human or environmental geography. The course focuses on using theory while encouraging the use of case studies. Course outcomes emphasise the development of conceptual and analytical skills.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Approaches and Issues in Physical and Environmental Sciences
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Issues and themes in contemporary aspects of the Physical/Environmental interface will be covered. Specific attention will be given to profiling core debates in a specialist field of physical or environmental geography. The course covers theoretical, empirical and methodological concerns and includes a fieldwork component.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The central question of this course is 'Why does adaptation to climate change need to be understood from a social and governance perspective?' Climate change adaptation is explored as a means for responding to global environmental change at local, national and international scales, and from the perspective of individuals, organisations and government. The course develops understanding of the complexity of adaptation and the relationship between reducing climate risk and broader socio-economic issues; how to position adaptation in the development context and as a means of transformation; and skills to critically assess adaptation responses and identify how social and governance aspects could be strengthened.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to the theory of geomorphological systems and applies this to an area or topic of the student's choice, particularly targeting Honours students who have selected physical geography topics for their dissertation. Students are expected to interpret landscapes, identify formative processes and events, examine environmental changes at different spatial and temporal scales, and place their area of study into geological, Quaternary, climatic and applied contexts, appreciating geomorphological concepts such as systems approach, complexity, relationships, feedbacks, thresholds, equilibrium and cycles.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course looks at the underlying dynamics involved in the negotiation of environmental policy and its implementation. The assumption is that unsustainable outcomes are not a result of a lack of will or intention, but rather due to vastly varying values, knowledge and data that are brought to bear on decision making for the environment. The course challenges the 'cultural embeddedness' of policy and critiques the cultural processes underlying environmental policy. Topics include the processes through which institutions define and mediate policy outcomes; governance arrangements for sustainable development; and the roles of power, rationality, knowledge and values in achieving environmental and social justice.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course explores urbanisation dynamics with a particular interest in examining the role of political economic shifts, history, discourse and new forms of techno-management in shaping the contemporary urban environment, drawing on urban political ecology as an interdisciplinary field that provides insights into power relations underlying unequal access to urban space, resources and infrastructure. The course aims to open conversations about the dynamics underlying unequal access to cities and the possibilities for more just and equitable cities. Students are expected to read set texts (empirical and theoretical) in preparation for weekly, student-led seminars.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS or cognate disciplines.
courses.course_outline: Located at the interface of fast-changing genetic and information technologies and a range of social, environmental and ethical concerns, the bio-economy has changed ways in which biodiversity is used, conserved and commercialised. Using a political ecology framing, this interdisciplinary course introduces key theories that situate the bio-economy and deepens understanding of emerging debates — from contestations about genetically modified crops and 'biopiracy' charges, to the potential of agroecology as a sustainable agricultural future. The course draws on research mostly from Sub-Saharan Africa and includes several short fieldtrips. Students are expected to read set texts, watch set videos and prepare seminars.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.
courses.assessment: 1 exam – 40%; 1 essay (3000–4000 words) – 30%; 1 seminar presentation – 15%; 1 critique (response to a particular article, policy or media piece) – 15%.

courses.course_title: Environmental Governance in the Global South
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to the theory and praxis of environmental governance relevant to global South contexts. It outlines dominant historical and current environmental governance theories, models and approaches at the global level, then engages economic, political, historical, institutional and social drivers that influence governance processes, using the global South as a lens. It draws on various natural resource use and governance sectors (biodiversity conservation, mining, forestry, tourism, etc.) and engages the Sustainable Development Goals from a governance perspective, critically analysing whether they can address issues relating to environmental governance practice in the global South.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Air Quality Monitoring, Management and Prediction
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course explores the current state of global air quality with a focus on examples and case studies from the global South, particularly Africa. Students investigate factors and role players influencing air quality, the distribution of polluted versus clean air, and impacts on people and the environment. The module looks at different scales of exposure to poor air quality and the data and tools used to study and monitor the atmosphere at these scales. Students also examine air quality management and the current socio-economic situation in South and southern Africa, culminating in a short research report with recommendations for interventions.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% class attendance and 40% minimum in assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Through a transdisciplinary lens, this course explores broad concepts, legislative and policy frameworks, practical applications, tools and skills influencing disaster risk science. The course begins by exploring different scholarly arguments about the unnatural state of natural disasters, then investigates theoretical models and case studies that underscore the fundamentals of disaster impact minimisation. It interrogates various ways in which humans, through environmental and socio-economic factors, drive vulnerability and subsequently create disaster risk.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Water Resources in a Changing Climate
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course investigates the evolving relationship between climate change, water resources and ecosystem resilience. Students critically analyse the effects of climate change on water systems and ecosystems, examine traditional and cutting-edge technological approaches to water resource management, and explore a variety of solutions including nature-based strategies, policy-driven approaches and engineered solutions. The course combines theoretical learning with practical application through face-to-face lectures, GIS and remote sensing exercises, fieldwork and interactive discussions.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance of all lectures and practicals, and mandatory field trips.
courses.assessment: Practicals 30%; group project 20%; group presentations 10%; final exam 40%.

courses.course_title: Reading the Sea: Interdisciplinary Approaches to Coastal Studies
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Drawing on South Africa's rich history of marine social science, the course introduces students to the study of the ocean from an interdisciplinary perspective. Students are familiarised with the main physical and biological features of South Africa's coastline, and investigate the diversity of ways in which coastal relations, livelihoods, communities and industries are operated, maintained and regulated through policy, practice and protest. The course exposes students to an array of marine social science approaches. Each week, students are required to read two readings, with a designated rapporteur leading class discussions. One guest lecture from researchers and/or practitioners is held every 3–4 weeks.
courses.dp_requirements: 80% attendance for class sessions. Final paper submitted, rapporteur session completed, and 2 out of 3 reflection pieces submitted.
courses.assessment: Reading journal 15%; class discussion 10%; reflection piece 15%; final essay 20%; exam 40%.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to the effects of climate change on marine and coastal ecosystems and the implications for marine ecosystem services. The course focuses on three themes: (i) changing marine and coastal environments in the face of climate change; (ii) linking biological responses to climate-induced drivers; and (iii) climate change impacts on marine ecosystem services.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.
courses.assessment: Research paper 25%; seminar presentation 20%; poster 15%; exam 40%.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.
courses.notes: This is a separate dissertation code to EGS5000W, covering an alternative Masters qualification pathway.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: An Honours degree (or equivalent). In special circumstances, graduates who have shown by examination, publication, or a record of appropriate training that they have reached a level equivalent to an Honours degree may be considered. Entrance limited to 12 students; selection is competitive, based on quality of qualification, experiential learning and/or referee reports.
courses.course_outline: This interdisciplinary course is designed for students with diverse backgrounds who have an interest in the environment, society and sustainability. This course contributes half of the total credits for a Masters qualification (awarded as MSc or MPhil depending on academic background). Coursework starts in February. Students select four modules from areas such as Theory & Practice of Environmental Management, Capital Politics & Nature, Geography of Development & Environment, Living with Environmental Change, Urban Food Security, Cultural Geographies, Managing Complex Human-Ecological Systems, or Geomorphology. Upon successful completion, students register for the minor dissertation (EGS5009W) in the following year.
courses.assessment: Assessment for coursework modules includes written examinations and coursework assignments (essays, projects, practical assignments, etc.). Examinations on average count 50% and coursework 50% for each module.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.semester: Full Year – registered in second year (W suffix)
courses.prerequisites: EGS5008H
courses.course_outline: Students register in the first semester of the second year and complete a suitable research proposal in consultation with an appropriate supervisor. After approval of the proposal, students undertake a research project demonstrating the application of theory to practical issues in environment, society and sustainability. The work must be submitted in the form of a minor dissertation early in the second year.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: A relevant Honours degree (or equivalent). Students with backgrounds in scientific, planning, engineering, economic, educational, social and legal disciplines are encouraged to apply.
courses.course_outline: This full-time taught Masters course (MSc or MPhil) is offered over 13 months beginning in January. It provides interdisciplinary training in climate change and sustainable development, with a focus on issues relevant to African development. The curriculum comprises two compulsory core courses — EGS5031F (Introduction to Climate Change & Sustainable Development) and EGS5032F/S (Climate Change Adaptation & Mitigation) — plus at least two elective courses from a range of modules allowing students to explore new areas or engage climate and development through existing disciplinary backgrounds.
courses.notes: Convened by UCT's African Climate & Development Initiative. The code EGS5012W represents the overall coursework component. A range of possible minor dissertation codes are available depending on the discipline in which the student chooses to register. Students may register for a minor dissertation in a range of Departments across the University.
courses.assessment: Students must pass all coursework with a sub-minimum of 33% for each core or elective course module; an aggregate coursework mark of 50% is required. The minor dissertation component counts 50% of the degree.

courses.course_title: Quantitative Research Methods for Environmental Earth Sciences
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS3012S or equivalent; OR at least one of a first-year Mathematics, Applied Mathematics, Physics or Computer Science course; OR at the discretion of the HOD.
courses.course_outline: The course is focussed on quantitative spatio-temporal analysis in the Earth Sciences, particularly tools commonly used in studies of the climate system, with emphasis on practical application and understanding of value and limitations. Classes are held in the EGS computer lab using Python on Linux. Analysis techniques include: cluster analysis; time series analysis including (auto)-correlation, trend, periodograms and wavelets; frequentist hypothesis testing and probability distributions; principal component analysis (PCA/EOF); and self-organising maps (SOMs).
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Senior Research Project in Environmental & Geographical Studies (MA)
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.course_outline: The minor dissertation is based on a three- to six-month supervised research project, to be submitted at the end of January, with the possibility of extension to June the following year.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS5012W
courses.course_outline: The minor dissertation is based on a three- to six-month supervised research project, to be submitted at the end of January, with the possibility of extension to June the following year.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: EGS4023F and EGS3012S or equivalent; OR at the discretion of the HoD.
courses.course_outline: The course provides an introduction to the fundamental elements of climate models and to the application of operational climate models and climate impact models to real-world questions, with applications focussed on the southern African context. Classes are held in the EGS computer lab using Python on Linux. Basic climate models — energy balance and advection-diffusion models — are developed from conceptual models to numerical implementation.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Introduction to Climate Change & Sustainable Development
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS5012W and by agreement of the course convenor.
courses.course_outline: This course provides broad, integrated knowledge on key issues in climate change and sustainable development. Topics include: sustainable development; the climate system, anthropogenic forcing and climate system response; African climate variability and change; international climate change legal frameworks, negotiations and politics; the economics of climate change and climate change financing; and the concept of climate compatible development. The course is lecture, seminar and group-work based.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS5012W and by agreement of the course convenor.
courses.course_outline: This course provides in-depth coverage of (i) adaptation and (ii) mitigation from both a theoretical and practical/applied point of view, from a developing country, climate compatible perspective. Adaptation and mitigation are the two key domains required for students to be qualified to undertake research and be employable in the climate change arena in the South African and developing country context.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The course explores the theory of climate change, then goes into the question of predictability, cross-scale relationships and feedbacks in the climate system, the tools and techniques of prediction, and translation of predictions into the user community including impacts and vulnerability analyses, touching on the social dimension. At NQF 9 level students are expected to compose reports at a higher intellectual level with a more comprehensive engagement with the pertinent literature and greater commitment in oral components.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS or cognate disciplines.
courses.course_outline: Topics include an overview of poverty and urbanization in Southern Africa; urban food security, methods and issues; urban poverty and vulnerability debates; food security and health; managing urban food systems (ecological, regulatory and fiscal dynamics). At NQF 9 level, students are expected to conduct a small piece of independent fieldwork informing an extended essay that critically engages a current debate on food security or food systems studies using a real-world case study.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Issues and themes in contemporary aspects of the Human/Environmental interface will be covered, profiling core debates in a specialist field of human or environmental geography. At NQF 9 level there is a strong emphasis on the development of analytical skills, and students are expected to apply these skills in the context of an appropriate theory to a case study of their own specialist field.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Approaches and Issues in Physical and Environmental Sciences
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: Issues and themes in contemporary aspects of the Physical/Environmental interface will be covered, profiling core debates in a specialist field of physical or environmental geography. The course covers theoretical, empirical and methodological concerns and includes a fieldwork component. At NQF 9 level there is strong emphasis on the development of analytical skills, with students expected to apply these skills to a case study of their own specialist field of physical geography.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to the theory of geomorphological systems applied to an area or topic of their choice. Students are expected to interpret landscapes, identify formative processes and events, examine environmental changes at different spatial and temporal scales, and appreciate key geomorphological concepts. At NQF 9 level, students are expected to back their literature review with data analyses (climatic or hydrological in nature), consider application of GIS and remote sensing, and convert elements of course content towards use in a publication.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: The module develops a comprehensive understanding of issues and challenges in water resources management at both an urban and catchment scale, with a primary focus on the South African context. Themes include increasing demand on scarce water resources, meeting the basic social need for clean potable water, consequences of interventions and institutional arrangements in water governance, and the role of the private sector in managing water risk. Topics include water quality, monitoring and compliance; new directions in water research in South Africa; biological treatment of water; participation in water governance; and corporate water stewardship. The course includes a three-week directed reading period and a 4-day field camp. At NQF 9 level, students complete an additional literature review assignment and prepare, manage and lead a course discussion.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course looks at the underlying dynamics involved in the negotiation of environmental policy and its implementation, challenging the 'cultural embeddedness' of policy. Topics include processes through which institutions define and mediate policy outcomes; governance arrangements for sustainable development; and the roles of power, rationality, knowledge and values in achieving environmental and social justice. At NQF 9 level, students are assigned two presentations and subsequent written submissions with emphasis on application of theoretical considerations, and an extended policy analysis assignment with additional analytical variables.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: A relevant Honours degree (or equivalent). Co-requisites: BIO5012W, BIO5013Z, BIO5014Z/SEA5011Z.
courses.course_outline: The minor dissertation, which forms 50% of the overall degree, is based on a six-month supervised research project. The choice of project will be determined by the student's prior qualification and in agreement with the course conveners and supervisors. The dissertation should be submitted at the end of January, with the possibility of extension to June of the next year.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course explores urbanisation dynamics with a particular interest in examining the role of political economic shifts, history, discourse and new forms of techno-management in shaping the contemporary urban environment, drawing on urban political ecology as an interdisciplinary field. The course aims to open conversations about the dynamics underlying unequal access to cities. Students are expected to read set texts in preparation for weekly, student-led seminars.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS or cognate disciplines.
courses.course_outline: Using a political ecology framing, this interdisciplinary course introduces key theories that situate the bio-economy and deepens understanding of emerging debates, from contestations about genetically modified crops and 'biopiracy' charges to the potential of agroecology as a sustainable agricultural future. The course draws on research mostly from Sub-Saharan Africa and includes a short fieldtrip. Students are expected to read set texts, watch set videos and prepare seminars.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.
courses.assessment: 1 exam – 40%; 1 essay (4000–5000 words) – 30%; 1 seminar presentation – 15%; 1 critique – 15%.

courses.course_title: Environmental Governance in the Global South
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for Honours or Master's specialising in EGS.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to the theory and praxis of environmental governance relevant to global South contexts, outlining dominant historical and current theories, models and approaches at the global level, and engaging various economic, political, historical, institutional and social drivers that influence governance processes. It also critically analyses the Sustainable Development Goals from a governance perspective in the context of the global South.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance record and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Air Quality Monitoring, Management and Prediction
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.course_outline: This course explores the current state of global air quality, with a focus on the global South and Africa. Students investigate factors and role players influencing air quality, the distribution of polluted versus clean air, and the impacts on people and the environment at different scales. Students identify an air quality issue of their choice, find and interpret relevant literature, appropriate datasets and methods, and produce results culminating in a short research report with recommendations for interventions.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% class attendance and 40% minimum in assignments.
courses.assessment: Literature review 10%; data analysis assignment and paper 35%; discussion piece 10%; project poster and presentation 15%; final capstone exam 30%.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Masters.
courses.course_outline: Through a transdisciplinary lens, this course explores broad concepts, legislative and policy frameworks, practical applications, tools and skills influencing disaster risk science. The course explores scholarly arguments about the unnatural state of natural disasters, investigates theoretical models and case studies that underscore the fundamentals of disaster impact minimisation, and interrogates various ways in which humans, through environmental and socio-economic factors, drive vulnerability and create disaster risk.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.

courses.course_title: Water Resources in a Changing Climate
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Masters.
courses.course_outline: This course investigates the evolving relationship between climate change, water resources and ecosystem resilience. Students critically analyse the effects of climate change on water systems and ecosystems, examine traditional and cutting-edge technological approaches to water resource management, and explore a variety of solutions. The course combines theoretical learning with practical application through face-to-face lectures, GIS and remote sensing exercises, fieldwork and interactive discussions.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance of all lectures and practicals, and mandatory field trips.
courses.assessment: Practicals 30%; group project 20%; group presentations 10%; final exam 40%.

courses.course_title: Reading the Sea: Interdisciplinary Approaches to Coastal Studies
courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Masters.
courses.course_outline: Drawing on South Africa's rich history of marine social science, this course introduces students to the study of the ocean from an interdisciplinary perspective. Students are familiarised with the main physical and biological features of South Africa's coastline, and investigate the diversity of ways in which coastal relations, livelihoods, communities and industries are operated, maintained and regulated through policy, practice and protest. Each week, students are required to read two readings, with a designated rapporteur leading class discussions.
courses.dp_requirements: 80% attendance for class sessions. Final paper submitted, rapporteur session completed, and 2 out of 3 reflection pieces submitted.
courses.assessment: Reading journal 15%; class discussion 10%; reflection piece 15%; final essay 20%; exam 40%.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.prerequisites: Acceptance for EGS Masters.
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to introduce students to the effects of climate change on marine and coastal ecosystems and the implications for marine ecosystem services. The course focuses on three themes: (i) changing marine and coastal environments in the face of climate change; (ii) linking biological responses to climate-induced drivers; and (iii) climate change impacts on marine ecosystem services.
courses.dp_requirements: At least 80% attendance and submission of all assignments.
courses.assessment: Research paper 25%; seminar presentation 20%; poster 15%; exam 40%.

courses.department: Department of Environmental and Geographical Science
courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative, demonstrating that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Prospective candidates are referred to the rules for this degree as set out in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
